title: Ombre gradient glaze dip on bisqueware

glossaryTerms.definition: clay that has been through the first firing (cone 06, approximately 1000 degrees C) and is now hard but unglazed and porous
a pyrometric measure of heat-work: cone 06 is approximately 1000 degrees C, cone 6 is approximately 1220 degrees C
the trimmed-back base ridge a pot sits on

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

An ombre gradient is created on bisqueware by dipping the piece at a slight angle into compatible glazes in sequence. Glaze sits thinner at one end and thicker at the other. The overlap zone blends into a gradual transition. The simplest version uses a single glaze. You do a full dip, then a partial re-dip to build thickness at the base. This produces a fade from light to deep in the same colour. The two-glaze version overlaps two colours in the middle zone. They bleed together at cone 6 (about 1220 degrees C) as the glazes melt and mix. The gradient is built at the dipping stage and revealed at the firing stage.
bisque

ware by dipping the piece at a slight angle into two compatible glazes in sequence, layering glaze thinner at one end and thicker at the other, so the overlap zone blends into a gradual transition. The most straightforward version uses a single glaze applied in a full dip followed by a partial re-dip to build thickness at the base, producing a fade from thin-to-thick of the same colour. The two-glaze version overlaps two colours in the middle zone, which bleed together at
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) as the glazes melt and intermix. The result is a gradient that is built at the dipping stage and revealed at the firing stage.
When to use it

Use the ombre dip on any bisque piece with a simple form where the gradient reads clearly from base to rim: cylinders, mugs, vases, and tall bowls all work well. The technique rewards simple forms: a complex surface breaks up the gradient and the transition zone becomes hard to read. Two compatible mid-fire glazes that move slightly during firing produce the most natural-looking blend at the overlap zone. Glazes that are very stiff and do not move at cone 6 produce a harder transition line rather than a gradient. Always test glaze compatibility on a small test tile before committing to a finished piece.
What you need

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed (colour A)
materials.quantity: enough to dip the full piece

content.text: Step by step

Wipe the bisqueware with a clean damp sponge and allow to dry for ten minutes. Ensure both glazes are stirred to an even consistency: test on the back of your hand by dipping briefly and observing whether the glaze coat is an even opaque layer approximately 1.5 mm thick. Adjust each glaze with water if it is too thick, or add a small amount of dry material if too thin. Clean the
foot ring
area on the piece with a sponge so it is free of dust.

For the single-colour fade: hold the piece by the foot ring and lower it straight down into glaze A until the entire exterior is submerged. Hold for three seconds. Lift cleanly. Allow to dry for five minutes until the glaze surface is dry to touch.

For the second dip (building the base gradient): re-dip the lower third of the piece into glaze A, tilting the piece at approximately 15 degrees to encourage a natural-looking diagonal boundary rather than a hard horizontal line. Hold for two seconds. Lift. Allow to dry. The base area now has two coats of glaze and will fire to a deeper version of the same colour. The transition between one coat and two coats softens during firing as the glaze melts.

For a two-colour gradient: apply glaze A to the full piece as above. Allow to dry. Apply glaze B to the lower third only by holding the piece at an angle and dipping to the desired gradient boundary. The overlap zone receives both glazes; they blend during firing where both layers are present.
Wipe the foot ring clean with a barely damp sponge. Check that no glaze has run to the very base of the piece. Leave to dry fully before loading the kiln.

Fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C). The overlap zone blends in the kiln as both glazes soften simultaneously. Place the piece on a well-kiln-washed shelf: the base area carries two layers of glaze and is more likely to drip than a single-dip piece.
What it looks like when it works

A successful single-colour ombre shows a clear transition from the rim to the base. The rim is lighter and more transparent where one thin coat sits. The base is deeper and more saturated where two coats overlapped. The boundary between the two dips is soft and diagonal, not hard and horizontal. A two-colour gradient shows a distinct first colour at the rim and a distinct second colour at the base. Between them is a blended zone where the two glazes have mixed. The width of that zone depends on how much the glazes flowed during firing.
Common mistakes

Hard horizontal line between the two dip zones: the piece was held level rather than slightly tilted during the second dip. A slight angle during dipping produces a more natural, softly diagonal boundary.
Glazes not blending at the overlap: the two glazes have very different surface tensions or melt behaviours at cone 6. Test glaze compatibility on a test tile before using on a finished piece.
Glaze crawling off the base: too much glaze at the base from three or more dips creates a layer that contracts and crawls during firing. Two dips at the base is the maximum for most glazes.

Drips running to the foot ring and fusing the piece to the kiln shelf: the double-glaze zone at the base is prone to running. Apply kiln wash to the shelf and leave a generous unglazed margin at the very base.
